text: Certain land was vested by Act of Parliament in the Royal A Society of New South Wales, for the purpose of holding agriculturs and exhibitions, and for other purposes, which were primarily an purposes of 'public recreation health or enjoyment," but with p the Society to charge the public for admission to the land, and to d profits made from the use of the land towards the objects of the These objects, as set out in the Society's Act of incorporation and ra in general such as came within the meaning of the exemption included also certain privileges for the members beyond those enjoyed general public. Held, that, as the members of the Society were entitled to a enjoyment from the land beyond that of the general public, the land within the exemption. 7 Decision of the Supreme Court, Municipal Council of Sydney Y. Agricultural Society of New South Wales, 5 S.R. (N.S.W reversed, Appeal from a decision of the Supreme Court. _— 3 CLR] OF AUSTRALIA. _ The respondents were assessed by the appellants for municipal rates in respect of a certain piece of land within the municipal boundaries of the Corporation of Sydney, under sec. 110 of the Sydney Corporation Act, 1902.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: The respondents appealed from the assessment to a Judge of the District Court. That learned Judge held that the respondents were not liable, on the ground that the land was vested in them as "trustees for purposes of public recreation health and enjoyment" within the meaning of » sec. 110, sub-sec. 5, of the Sydney Corporation Act, 1902. From that decision the appellants appealed by way of special case stated for the opinion of the Supreme Court, and that Court dismissed . the appeal with costs: Municipal Council of Sydney v.

text: Gordon, K.C., (Leverrier with him), for the appellants. The purposes for which the land is vested in the respondents are set " out in sec. 4 of the Act No. 45 of 1902. Assuming that the res1 pondents are trustees, the purposes for which it is vested in them _ are not necessarily "for public recreation health or enjoyment." The purposes must be exclusively of that kind, just as, in the case . of land exempted by reason of its being used for public purposes, the land must be used exclusively for those purposes: Mayor ce. of Essenden v.

text: Blackwood (2). The purposes actually enumerated in the section, down to the words "live stock," might possibly be within the exemption, but the Minister has power to sanction the use of the land for "other purposes." The "other purposes" are not necessarily ejusdem generis with those mentioned. The " only restriction on the respondents is that pony racing is not to be allowed.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: Where a class or classes of purposes are specified, _ other purposes must mean purposes of another class. The doctrine _ of ejusdem generis only applies to cases where the objects men_ tioned are particular, not generic. Sec. 5 does not revoke the vesting upon the happening of the events stated; it merely gives power to revoke.

Attorney-General for New South Wales (2); and "trustee" in that Act means a trustee in whom a place of public recreation, convenience, health or enjoyment is vested by law.

text: That is sufficient to bring them within the meaning of sec. 110 of the Corporation Act, for the terms of the Statute No. 45 of 1902, under which the respondents now hold, are in effect the same as those of the lease under which they originally held from the Council. If the land is not now vested in the respondents for the exempted purposes it is in the Council. In either case it is exempt.

text: The respondents have no power under the Act No. 45 of 1902 to derive a beneficial enjoyment from the land: sec. 3 and the preamble. All the objects mentioned in the Act are public, and _ there is no power to make rules inconsistent with those objects, and, So far as the rules exceed the power, they are void. The user of the land is immaterial.

text: This is an appeal from a decision of the December. apres Court affirming a decision of the Metropolitan District ourt on an appeal by the respondent Society from an assessment the appellants under the Sydney Corporation Act, in respect of a municipal rate. The learned District Court Judge held that _the respondents were not ratable, and in that opinion the Supreme concurred. ; 'The question depends upon the proper construction of sec. 110 s of the particular case. That Act was a re-enactment of ier Acts, sec. 110 having been taken from the Act 43 Vict.

text: No. It is contained in Part IX., which deals with General Rates. b-sec. 4 of that section provides that : " Every building, whether d in or occupied by the Crown or not, and all lands, whether pied or not, within the city, save as hereinafter mentioned, be deemed to be 'ratable property' within the meaning of s Act." Sub-sec. 5 provides, inter alia that: "No land vested trustees for purposes of public recreation, health or enjoyment P shall be liable to be assessed or rated in respect of any rate under this Act." text: HIGH COURT The respondents claimed that the land in question vested in them under circumstances to which I will p refer, is vested in them as trustees for the purposes of recreation, health or enjoyment, and is therefore exe rating. Now, the first question is what is the meaning of the e 'pI "trustees for purposes of public recreation, health or ment?" Primd facie, bearing in mind that this is a rati this expression relates to land vested in persons haying no ficial interest in the land, but holding it solely for the pu public recreation, health or enjoyment. An implied term it a trust would be that any money which the trustees 1 from the use of the land by the general public should be aj for the purposes of the trust, that is, in expenditure upoi land, for the purpose of making it better available objects of the trust.

text: Nor could it be said, primd fu persons who have a beneficial use of the land, subject t condition that they shall allow it to be used for the p public recreation, health, or enjoyment, would properly be ¢ trustees of the land for those purposes. An analogous matt this was dealt with by the Privy Council in 1877, in the ¢ The Mayor &c. of Essenden vy. Blackwood (1), on appeal fi Supreme Court of Victoria.

text: The question in that case was ¥ the Flemington Racecourse was exempt from rating, as land property of the Crown used for publie purposes. In ordi sustain the exemption, it was necessary, in the view their L ships took of the matter, to establish both that the land w property of Her Majesty, and that it was used for public pury The Judicial Committee did not decide the first point, whethe land was the property of Her Majesty, but confined its att to the question whether it was used for public purposes wil ; meaning of the section conferring the exemption. The la held by the trustees of the Victorian Racing Club, which wasi porated by a private Act, and the members of the Club had var privileges in respect of the land.

And then it was pointed out at, though in the old cases it was supposed that land used for ic purposes, and from which the occupiers derived no individual fit, was exempt from ratability, it was nevertheless always I that all the purposes must be public. text: In September, 1881, they ast executed a lease of the area now in question to the re (1) A.C., 444. a "3 CLR) OF AUSTRALIA. for the purposes only of shows or exhibitions, and the respondents undertook to keep the land drained and cleaned and to comply ith the Council's regulations as to access by the public. The question was then raised whether the lease by the Council, . who were trustees of the land which was held by the Privy Council to be dedicated for the purposes of public recreation, health or enjoyment under the Public Parks Act, was valid or _ inyalid. The Chief Judge in Equity held that the lease was bad and declared accordingly, but the Privy Council on appeal were _ of opinion that there was no objection to the lease.

text: Before that time the present respondents had been incorporated _ by an Act of 1869, which is not numbered, and I assume, therefore, is to be taken as in one sense a private Act. It recited that a society called the Agricultural Society of New South Wales had been formed with certain rules and by-laws at Sydney for the encouragement and improvement of agriculture, and for promoting _ the success of pastoral and farming pursuits in the colony, and also for holding exhibitions of live stock, of agricultural, horticultural, and pastoral produce, of minerals, and of arts and manufactures. It then proceeded to declare that the President, Vice-Presidents, Governors, and members of the Society for the time being should be incorporated by the name of the " Agricul_ tural Society of New South Wales," with all the privileges and liabilities of a corporation.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: It then provided that the then present tules and by-laws should be the rules and by-laws of the corporation, save and except so far as they might be lawfully altered or repealed or might be inconsistent or incompatible with or repugnant to any of the provisions of the Act or the laws of the colony. The sixth section provided that the corporation should have power to purchase, acquire, and hold lands, and any interest therein, and to sell and dispose of them, and that all lands then the property of the Society should become the property of the corporation. There was also a provision that, in the event of the funds and property of the corporation being insufficient to meet its engagements, each member should be liable to contribute, over and above his annual subseription, a sum equal to the subscription, towards liquidating the liabilities of the corporation, and should not be otherwise individually liable.

The lands so acquired may clearly be anyw! New South Wales. Sub-sec. 4 mentions the holding of "e tions in Sydney or elsewhere for the display of horses, catt sheep, and other live stock; wool, agricultural produce of all kin and machinery ; together with such other subjects of manufaet produce, or the arts as may be deemed desirable ; and to estab in connection therewith a market for the sale of exhibited stock, machinery," &. Sub-sec. 5 includes the encouragement skill in farm labour, and showing new machinery in operatic and so bringing it into notice, and sub-sec. 6 provides for publication and distribution amongst the members of # and kindred societies, of the transactions of the Society. | much for the objects of the Society. The privileges, amor other things, include, as appears from rule 7, the right to vote elections of office bearers, of free admission to the library reading room, and to the Society's exhibitions and other en' ments held in the grounds, on production of the member's for the current year. It is a matter of common knowledge in institutions of this kind the privileges of members are s times very considerable, and much more comfortable accommo tion is provided for them than for other persons. But whe the privileges are small or great, it is clear that the membersh a beneficial enjoyment of the property as distinguished from t general public. a 4] OF AUSTRALIA. is was the Society to which the Municipal Council granted se of the land for a period to last during the will of the suncil, or until notice given as provided in the lease.

text: One asks then what is the nature of the interest of the Society in e land? A piece of land is given them by the legislature for certain purposes, with express power to receive money for the admission of the public, and without any control upon the expenditure of these profits except that they are not to do or suffer anything whereby the land should become vested in any other person or persons. I think the cases clearly show that under those cireumstances the members of the Society had a beneficial enjoyment of the land beyond that of the general public, There was a good deal of discussion as to the words "such other purposes as the Minister may sanction," and it was said that they must mean purposes ejusdem generis with those mentioned.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: But this argument was not very strongly pressed in this Court, though it was favoured in the Supreme Court. If the only other purposes were shows and exhibitions of that kind, there might be some force in the contention. But it cannot be disputed, since the decision in Sydney Municipal Council v.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: Attorney-General for JV.S.W. (1), that the land might be used for any purposes of a quasi public nature as distinguished from private. As a matter of fact the Society derived a very considerable rental from letting a portion of the ground for the use of the mounted police of Sydney for stabling purposes. It was suggested that that might be ultra vires, but it is not necessary to determine in this case, what limit, if any, must be put on the meaning of the words "other purposes." It is sufficient, in the view I take of the case, to say that the 'members as such had conferred upon them by the legislature a beneficial enjoyment of this land.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: They are very much in the position of lessees whose lease is subject to restrictive covenants. The restrictions are certainly great, but the lease is a beneficial one notwithstanding. For these reasons I think that they are not trustees of the land for the purposes of public recreation, health or enjoyment within the meaning of sec. 110 of the Sydney Corporation Act.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: The learned Judge who delivered the judgment of the Supreme Court said that he understood that the point that the Society were trustees of the lands for purposes of public recreation, health (1) A.C., 444. H. C. or A, 1905. —— MonicrraL CounciL or Sypney v.

text: HIGH COURT or enjoyment was conceded. If that were so, of cours would clearly be within the meaning of the exemption, a exemption would not be taken away by a mere misuse of and I quite agree with the Supreme Court in that respe the question is what was the nature of the title of the If they commit a breach of their trust, the remedy is provi sec. 5 of the Act, and also by the general law applicable to tra But we are assured that this is a misapprehension, becaus really the only point in the case. The whole question of liability depends on the nature of their tenure, that is, wh they are trustees for such purposes or not.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: For the have given I think that this land does not come wit exemption. Reference was made to the use of the word "solely" in the section of the Sydney Corporation Act with reference to h and buildings used for public worship. But that is not s to change what is, im my opinion, the clear construction of words "vested in trustees for the purposes of public recreat health and enjoyment." For these reasons I think the appeal from the District Co should have been allowed, and that this appeal shot allowed.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: Barton J. I agree that the appeal should be allowed, for the reasons that the Chief Justice has given. O'Connor J.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: I am of the same opinion. On one part of the case I should like to add a few words what has been said by the Chief Justice. I do not think necessary to consider whether it is within the power of Minister under the words "for such other purposes as_ Minister may sanction," to authorize the use of this land f purpose which is not one of public recreation, health, or enj ment, within the meaning of sec. 110 of the Sydney Corpo Act.

text: The consideration of that matter may involve diff questions which it is not necessary now to enter upon, on the other ground it appears to me that the appellants h made out a very clear case. text: Attorney-General for N.S.W. (1), commonly known as pee Milroy's Case, the holding of shows and exhibitions on this Wats. Jand is a purpose of public "recreation, health and enjoyment." o'connor 3. But that does not settle the matter, because according to the rule laid down in Mayor &e. of Essenden v.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: Blackwood (2), for the interpretation of a section of this kind, there is no exemption from taxation unless the lands are vested in trustees solely for these purposes. Dr. Cullen endeavoured to distinguish the _ words of exemption in that case from those of sub-see. 5 of sec. 110 inthis case.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: But I find it impossible to see any distinction. The words in that case were " lands the property of Her Majesty occupied or used for public purposes." It was held there that the words must be considered as meaning used solely for public _purposes. In the present case the words in sec. 110, " vested in trustees for purposes of public recreation, health, or enjoyment," ~ seem to me to come exactly within the same principle, and must be construed in the same way.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: The English decisions on the subject of rating are not, as has "been already pointed out, altogether applicable to circumstances similar to those now under consideration. But, according to the principle which was followed by the Priv case to which I have just referred, one element for consideration Council in the "always is, to what purposes is the money produced by the use of * the land devoted. If it is not devoted altogether to public pur* poses or trusts, then there is a beneficial interest in the members, which has always been held sufficient to prevent the exemption "applying.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

Harrogate (4), "you have to shew 'that all the purposes to which the money was applied are public.' (1) A.C., 444. (3) 2 App. Cas., 574, at p. 588. (2) 2 App.

text: Connor J. HIGH COURT Now. in applying that test, we must see not only that is used for the purposes of the Society, but that it is vested for the purposes of public recreation, health and enjoym: solely for those purposes, and also that the proceeds whie derived by the Society from the use of the land are to be dey also to the purposes of public recreation, health and enjoy To determine this question it is only necessary to look at. constitution of the Society. It is constituted by its own Act, for a variety of purposes, which are in one sense public. learned brother the Chief Justice has explained in detail what th purposes are.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: It is quite clear that all moneys received byt Society may be devoted to any of those purposes, The land in question is vested in the Royal Agri Society by the Act No. 45 of 1902, (I do not think it at present to refer to the earlier Acts), for a period of twenty years subject to certain conditions. These conditions are y definite. One stated in sec. 4, is that the Society may oceuj and use the land for the purposes of holding shows of agriculit and pastoral produce, stock, machinery, arts, manufactures, such other purposes as the Minister may sanction, ani charges for admission, provided that certain race meetings are to be held there. The next condition is in the next sul that the Society are to keep the land clean and free from off matter, that is to say, in a sanitary condition.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: The next subis a restriction on selling, mortgaging or dealing with or of the land. In the latter part of the same sub-section it provided that if they shall do anything whereby any part of land or of the profits thereof shall vest in or become pay: to any other person, or if the Society should cease to exist, land shall upon notification in the Gazette revest in His Maj These are the only conditions imposed upon their dealing the land,and the only conditions which restrict their right to disp of the profits derived from its occupation in any way they th fit. Clearly they have the right, so long as they do not infring any of these conditions, to dispose of the profits in any way # please in carrying on the operations of the Society.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: It is quite that we have to consider, not what use they made of the land, but what purposes it is vested in them, taking them to be trustees f¢ 3OLR.] OF AUSTRALIA. 315 the purposes of sec. 110 of the Sydney Corporation Act. The H.-C. pres terms on which the land is vested in these trustees are to be as ascertained entirely from the Act vesting it in them and from the yoyrcrpan "Act by which the Society itself is incorporated. And, as there is power under the Society's Act to apply any of the profits derived pe aia "from the occupation of this ground to the purposes mentioned in cunruran that Act, it appears to me that there is an obligation imposed to pepe apply these profits to purposes, which, although in themselves Wa¥5*. public ina sense, are not the public purposes set out in sec. 110 of o'connor J. the Sydney Corporation Act.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: Inasmuch as this land is to be 'applied to the purposes of the Society, which are not purposes of public recreation, health or enjoyment, it seems to me impossible, in "view of the decision in the case of Mayor «te. of Essenden v. * Blackwood (1), to hold that the Society have brought themselves 'within this exception. When the expression "beneficial interest " is used, it is not intended to mean a personal interest. There may be a beneficial interest, as was pointed out by the Chief 'Justice, in the sense of a privilege or privileges enjoyed by the members, beyond those enjoyed by the general public.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56

text: If the 'Society has such an interest in the land that it may apply the fits derived from it to its own purposes, which though public 'in one sense, are not enjoyed equally by the public in general, »then there is a beneficial interest such as will prevent the application of the exemption in sub-sec. 5 of sec. 110 of the Sydney Corporation Act. For these reasons I am of opinion that the decision of the 'Supreme Court was erroneous, and that this appeal should be ni ® Appeal allowed with costs. Judgments of the District Court and Supreme Court reversed.
citation: Sydney Municipal Council v Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales [1905] HCA 56
